summary: Spolsky argues that software options dialogs represent a failure of design, where designers abdicate their responsibility to make decisions by pushing choices onto users. He uses the analogy of prohibitive signs in restaurants being historical markers of past problems, comparing them to software options that memorialize unresolved internal debates. Through examples like the Windows Help indexing wizard and draggable toolbars, he demonstrates how unnecessary choices confuse users and create accidental misconfigurations. He concludes that designers should minimize decisions users must make, reserving choices only for things users actually care about — their work output and cosmetic preferences.
key_points: Options dialogs are archaeological evidence of unresolved design debates — each option represents a decision the team couldn't or wouldn't make themselves.
Every option forces a decision on the user, and most users care far less about program configuration than designers assume — they care about their task, not the tool's internals.
Customizability can actively harm users when accidental reconfigurations leave them in states they don't understand and can't undo, like dragging a menu bar or Windows taskbar out of position.
Even power users rarely customize because settings don't propagate across machines, reinstalls, and upgrades — the effort isn't worth it.

Good design reserves choices for things integral to the user's actual work (document appearance, website behavior) or purely cosmetic preferences (skins, wallpapers), not internal implementation details.
money_quotes: Every time you provide an option, you're asking the user to make a decision.
It is the height of arrogance for a software designer to inflict a choice like this on the user simply because the designer couldn't think hard enough to decide which option is really better.
Pull up the Tools | Options dialog box and you will see a history of arguments that the software designers had about the design of the product.
The trouble with customizing your environment is that it just doesn't propagate, so it's not even worth the trouble.
When you are designing, and you try to abdicate your responsibility by forcing the user to decide something, you're probably not doing your job.
It's sort of shocking how many people uninstall and then reinstall their software when things start behaving wrong, because at least they know how to do that.
Some annoying help-index-engine-programmer at Microsoft with an inflated idea of his own importance to the whole scheme of things has the audacity, the chutzpah, to interrupt the user once again.
key_insight: Design is the art of making choices on behalf of users — every option you expose is a confession that you failed to decide, and most users neither want nor benefit from that burden.
